curriculum_source: CFA Program 2027 Level III, Volume 5 (Ethical and Professional Standards), Learning Module 9: Application of the Code and Standards: Level III

reading.reading_name: Application of the Code and Standards: Level III

drills.los_text: evaluate practices, policies, and conduct relative to the CFA Institute Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct
drills.reading_name: Application of the Code and Standards: Level III

questions.stem: *SIC Case — Adebayo decision to invest in urban infrastructure project rather than mining project*: quickly decides NI should invest in urban project before mining project; sequencing offers benefit of supporting current party in power and postponing mining until opposition may gain control; also believes urban infrastructure may be more profitable short-term. *Most likely* Adebayo's decision violates:

options.A: Standard V(A) Diligence and Reasonable Basis — Adebayo's decision to invest 'quickly' without apparent analysis is made based on POLITICAL, not investment or financial, considerations.
options.B: There is no violation because sovereign investment decisions are government policy choices outside the Code.
options.C: Adebayo violated only Standard III(C) Suitability, since the urban project fits NI's socioeconomic mandate.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Standard V(A) Diligence and Reasonable Basis — Adebayo's decision to invest 'quickly' without apparent analysis is made based on POLITICAL, not investment or financial, considerations; Adebayo is facing political pressures from senior governmental supporters of each project; she must thoroughly and independently investigate each potential investment and be resolute in maintaining her independence and objectivity to act in the best interests of NI and its investors.

Correct. Per SIC Case Solution: 'Standard V(A) Diligence and Reasonable Basis requires members to exercise diligence, independence, and thoroughness, as well as to have a reasonable and adequate basis to support investment action. Adebayo's decision to invest "quickly" without apparent analysis is made based on political, not investment or financial, considerations. Adebayo is facing political pressures from senior governmental supporters of each project. She must thoroughly and independently investigate each potential investment and be resolute in maintaining her independence and objectivity to act in the best interests of NI and its investors.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. NI's investment staff remain bound by the Standards; a quick, politically driven decision without analysis fails V(A).
rationales.C: Incorrect. The failure is DILIGENCE — deciding without investment analysis — not mandate fit.

questions.stem: *SIC Case — Adebayo's assurances to hedge fund partners*: Corrales asks Adebayo for detailed info; Adebayo gives standard investor disclosure package; hedge fund partners want assurances NI will maintain aggressive investment strategy and commitment to high returns; Adebayo assures them NI is committed to providing investors with high rates of return. *Most likely* Adebayo violates:

options.A: There is no violation because providing the standard investor disclosure package satisfied all duties to HFI.
options.B: Standard III(C) Suitability — in the case of NI, Adebayo has a DUAL MANDATE to invest in projects that have both a beneficial socioeconomic impact and a positive expected investment return.
options.C: Adebayo violated only Standard I(C) by overstating expected returns to the hedge fund partners.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Assuring high returns ignores NI's DUAL MANDATE (socioeconomic impact plus expected return) — a III(C) failure beyond disclosure mechanics.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Standard III(C) Suitability — in the case of NI, Adebayo has a DUAL MANDATE to invest in projects that have both a beneficial socioeconomic impact and a positive expected investment return; this dual mandate is in contrast to traditional investment mandates that focus only on ROI regardless of outside or other impact; NI's investors are informed up front that investment return is not the single determining factor for NI's investment decisions; any assurances by Adebayo to the hedge fund or other investors that promise a SET RATE OF RETURN or PRIORITIZE RETURN MAXIMIZATION would be contrary to NI's mandate.

Correct. Per SIC Case Solution: 'Standard III(C) Suitability requires CFA Institute members responsible for managing assets to a specific mandate, strategy, or style to take only investment actions that are consistent with that mandate. In the case of NI, Adebayo has a dual mandate to invest in projects that have both a beneficial socioeconomic impact and a positive expected investment return... Any assurances by Adebayo to the hedge fund or other investors that promise a set rate of return or prioritize return maximization would be contrary to NI's mandate.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The tested violation is suitability under the dual mandate, not a bare misstatement.

questions.curriculum_ref: V5 LM9 — SIC Case (Adebayo hedge fund)

questions.stem: *SIC Case — Corrales investment for personal portfolio*: after HFI decides to invest in NI, Corrales immediately invests 25% of his personal portfolio in NI in return for shares before the hedge fund invests; after HFI investment made public, NI shares trade higher on exchange. *Most likely* Corrales violates:

options.A: There is no violation because Corrales invested personal capital and took personal risk in NI.
options.B: Corrales's conduct is fully addressed by Standard VI(A) as an undisclosed conflict of interest.
options.C: Standard VI(B) Priority of Transactions — Corrales engaged in the unethical and often illegal practice of FRONT-RUNNING, or trading in advance for one's personal account prior to trading for client accounts for economic advantage.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Buying ahead of the fund's known pending investment is FRONT-RUNNING — personal risk-taking does not excuse it.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Disclosure would not cure it; trading ahead of client transactions is a PRIORITY violation under VI(B).
rationales.C:
Full statement: Standard VI(B) Priority of Transactions — Corrales engaged in the unethical and often illegal practice of FRONT-RUNNING, or trading in advance for one's personal account prior to trading for client accounts for economic advantage; Standard VI(B) states investment transactions for clients must have priority over investment transactions for personal benefit; this practice is unethical and violates Standard VI(B) EVEN IF his personal investment does not disadvantage the hedge fund in any way; Corrales should have waited to invest in NI until AFTER the hedge fund had completed its investment in NI.

Correct. Per SIC Case Solution: 'In this instance, Corrales engaged in the unethical and often illegal practice of front-running, or trading in advance for one's personal account prior to trading for client accounts for economic advantage. Standard VI(B) Priority of Transactions states that investment transactions for clients must have priority over investment transactions for personal benefit. After the hedge fund invests in NI, the value of NI shares increases, benefiting Corrales through an increase in the value of his personal investment. This practice is unethical and violates Standard VI(B) even if his personal investment does not disadvantage the hedge fund in any way. Corrales should have waited to invest in NI until after the hedge fund had completed its investment in NI.'

questions.stem: *SIC Case — Corrales subadviser 'deal fees' reported as operating expenses*: local subadvisers have minimal experience but close connections with local high-ranking officials; pay 'deal fees' to cover expenses to facilitate governmental support; Corrales does not require details of activities; reports as operating expenses to hedge fund investors. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: Standard I(C) Misrepresentation — by reporting subadvisory payments as legitimate hedge fund 'operating expenses,' Corrales is MISREPRESENTING the nature of the payments to hide illegal activity.
options.B: The failure is one of diligence and reasonable basis only, since the subadvisers lack investment experience.
options.C: The failure concerns independence and objectivity, since the officials' connections influenced the selection.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Standard I(C) Misrepresentation — by reporting subadvisory payments as legitimate hedge fund 'operating expenses,' Corrales is MISREPRESENTING the nature of the payments to hide illegal activity; Corrales is essentially engaged in BRIBERY and facilitating local corruption; the 'deal fees' are corrupt transactions and are not supported by any documentation that details legitimate investment expenses; Corrales very likely violates Standard I(A) Knowledge of the Law because his conduct would most likely violate applicable anti-bribery laws.

Correct. Per SIC Case Solution: 'By reporting subadvisory payments as legitimate hedge fund "operating expenses," Corrales is misrepresenting the nature of the payments to hide illegal activity and is in violation of Standard I(C) Misrepresentation. Although it is acceptable to hire subadvisers to assist in sourcing investment opportunities, Corrales is not hiring a subadviser to assist in the investment decision-making process but is essentially engaged in bribery and facilitating local corruption. Such conduct would violate multiple standards... Corrales very likely violates Standard I(A) Knowledge of the Law because his conduct would most likely violate applicable anti-bribery laws.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The inexperience matters, but reporting connection payments as 'operating expenses' MISREPRESENTS the fund's costs — I(C), with I(A) implicated by the arrangement's nature.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The tested violation is misrepresenting the payments in reporting, not the analyst's own objectivity.

questions.stem: *SIC Case — Adebayo continues using CFA designation*: Mehrotra notices Adebayo continues to use CFA designation on business card and marketing materials despite not paying dues; Adebayo tells him credential is like a university degree that cannot be taken away; during sales presentation Adebayo says NI's senior investment professionals are all charterholders. *Most likely* Adebayo violates:

options.A: There is no violation because Adebayo legitimately earned the charter by passing all three exams.
options.B: Standard VII(B) Reference to CFA Institute, the CFA Designation, and the CFA Program — Adebayo has not been paying dues, so her CFA Institute membership is considered LAPSED.
options.C: Adebayo's conduct violates only Standard I(A) as noncompliance with CFA Institute rules.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Passing the exams is not enough — unpaid dues lapse the membership, ending the right to present-tense designation use.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Standard VII(B) Reference to CFA Institute, the CFA Designation, and the CFA Program — Adebayo has not been paying dues, so her CFA Institute membership is considered LAPSED; until membership is reactivated, she must NOT use the CFA designation or present herself as a charterholder to potential investors, although she MAY state she was previously a CFA Institute member; the CFA designation is NOT like a university degree — individuals must satisfy CFA Institute membership requirements (including paying dues and submitting a PCS annually) to maintain the right to refer to themselves as charterholders.

Correct. Per SIC Case Solution (Mehrotra): 'Standard VII(B) Reference to CFA Institute, the CFA Designation, and the CFA Program states that when referring to the CFA designation, CFA Institute members and candidates "must not misrepresent... holding the designation." The CFA designation is not like a degree from a university in that once granted the right to use the designation, individuals must also satisfy CFA Institute membership requirements (including paying dues and submitting a Professional Conduct Statement annually) to maintain the right to refer to themselves as CFA charterholders. Although Adebayo earned her charter, her membership is considered lapsed because she has not been paying her CFA Institute dues. Until her membership is reactivated, she must not use the CFA designation or present herself as a charterholder to potential investors, although she may state she was previously a CFA Institute member.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Misrepresenting active charterholder status is squarely VII(B).

questions.curriculum_ref: V5 LM9 — SIC Case (Adebayo CFA use)

questions.stem: *SIC Case — Mehrotra silence when Adebayo misrepresents to client*: Mehrotra knows Adebayo's membership has lapsed but stays silent when Adebayo tells client that NI's senior investment professionals are all CFA charterholders. *Most likely* Mehrotra violates:

options.A: There is no violation because Mehrotra himself made no false statement to the client.
options.B: Mehrotra's silence implicates only Standard VII(A), as conduct compromising the designation's integrity.
options.C: Standard I(A) Knowledge of the Law — prohibits members from knowingly participating or assisting in the violations of others and requires members to dissociate from any unethical or illegal conduct.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Knowing silence while a colleague misrepresents to a client is knowing PARTICIPATION or assistance in the violation — I(A) requires dissociation.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The operative failure is I(A) knowledge-of-the-law participation, not program conduct.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Standard I(A) Knowledge of the Law — prohibits members from knowingly participating or assisting in the violations of others and requires members to dissociate from any unethical or illegal conduct; Mehrotra's ACQUIESCENCE AND SILENCE during Adebayo's misrepresentation to the client, at minimum, assists Adebayo's violation of the standard; by keeping silent about false information given to a potential investor, when doing so is potentially harmful to that investor, Mehrotra's behavior constitutes ASSISTING in the violations of those who provide that false information even if there is NO ACTIVE CONDUCT by Mehrotra.

Correct. Per SIC Case Solution: 'Standard I(A) Knowledge of the Law prohibits members from knowingly participating or assisting in the violations of others and requires members to dissociate from any unethical or illegal conduct. Mehrotra's acquiescence and silence during Adebayo's misrepresentation to the client, at minimum, assists Adebayo's violation of the standard. By keeping silent about false information given to a potential investor, when doing so is potentially harmful to that investor, Mehrotra's behavior constitutes assisting in the violations of those who provide that false information even if there is no active conduct by Mehrotra.'

questions.stem: *SIC Case — Mehrotra soliciting exam info from students*: at post-exam celebration, Mehrotra asks students their opinion on the most difficult exam questions, hoping to use this information to help ensure future students are better prepared. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: Standard VII(A) Conduct as Participants in CFA Institute Programs — candidates cannot provide specific information about the exam regarding the questions or the subjects tested within a topic area.
options.B: There is no violation because gathering post-exam feedback is standard practice for exam-prep instructors.
options.C: Mehrotra's conduct violates only Standard I(A) as a breach of CFA Institute's terms of service.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Standard VII(A) Conduct as Participants in CFA Institute Programs — candidates cannot provide specific information about the exam regarding the questions or the subjects tested within a topic area; Mehrotra violated Standard VII(A) by SOLICITING information about specific exam questions.

Correct. Per SIC Case Solution: 'Candidates cannot provide specific information about the exam regarding the questions or the subjects tested within a topic area. Mehrotra violated Standard VII(A) by soliciting information about specific exam questions.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Soliciting specific question information for prep-class use targets confidential exam content — VII(A) prohibits it, whatever the industry habit.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Program-integrity conduct is governed by VII(A) directly.

questions.stem: *SIC Case — Mehrotra sharing students' opinions on exam difficulty*: sharing his students' opinions about the difficulty of the exam to candidates in future preparatory classes to emphasize the need to thoroughly prepare. *Most likely*:

options.A: Sharing students' difficulty opinions violates VII(A) because all post-exam commentary is confidential.
options.B: NO violation — candidates may generally disclose whether they believed the CFA exam was difficult relative to their expectations.
options.C: Difficulty opinions may never be relayed to future candidates in any educational setting.

rationales.A: Incorrect. GENERAL difficulty impressions are permitted commentary; only specific content is confidential.
rationales.B:
Full statement: NO violation — candidates may generally disclose whether they believed the CFA exam was difficult relative to their expectations; it is ACCEPTABLE for Mehrotra to advise future preparatory classes that his previous students found the CFA exam to be more difficult than expected and thus students should study the curriculum and prepare as much as possible.

Correct. Per SIC Case Solution: 'Candidates may generally disclose whether they believed the CFA exam was difficult relative to their expectations. It is acceptable for Mehrotra to advise future preparatory classes that his previous students found the CFA exam to be more difficult than expected and thus students should study the curriculum and prepare as much as possible.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Relaying general difficulty views to future classes is acceptable — it conveys no exam content.

questions.stem: *BankGlobal Case — Hockett's team acting on rec changes for discretionary accounts before public dissemination*: team acts on recommendation changes for discretionary accounts before changes are publicly disseminated to all clients. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: There is no violation because discretionary authority allows the team to trade at any time.
options.B: The team's trading implicates only Standard II(A), because a pending recommendation is inside information.
options.C: Standard III(B) Fair Dealing — members and candidates must deal fairly and objectively with all clients when providing investment analysis, making recs, taking action, or engaging in other professional activities.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Discretion governs authority, not sequence — acting for some accounts before dissemination denies other clients fair treatment.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The firm's own recommendation is not issuer MNPI; the failure is FAIR DEALING under III(B).
rationales.C:
Full statement: Standard III(B) Fair Dealing — members and candidates must deal fairly and objectively with all clients when providing investment analysis, making recs, taking action, or engaging in other professional activities; Hockett's team is not treating all BankGlobal clients in a fair and impartial manner; Hockett is giving PREFERENTIAL TREATMENT to his discretionary accounts at the expense of other clients.

Correct. Per BankGlobal Case Solution: 'By acting on the analysts' recommendation changes for their discretionary accounts before the changes are publicly disseminated, the team is violating Standard III(B) Fair Dealing. Standard III(B) requires members and candidates to "deal fairly and objectively with all clients when providing investment analysis, making investment recommendations, taking investment action, or engaging in other professional activities."'

questions.stem: *BankGlobal Case — Kochanski meeting recommending balanced portfolio*: Hockett and Lopez meet with Kochanskis (61, retired, $7.4M); at meeting Kochanskis share they don't know enough about markets to manage own investments; Hockett/Lopez go through presentation and explain why balanced portfolio best suited for them. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: Standard III(C) Suitability — by developing and recommending a balanced portfolio for the Kochanskis WITHOUT KNOWING their clients' investment objectives, risk tolerance, horizon, and so on.
options.B: There is no violation because a balanced portfolio is inherently reasonable for a retired couple.
options.C: The failure is one of performance presentation under Standard III(D), since the meeting centered on fund returns.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Standard III(C) Suitability — by developing and recommending a balanced portfolio for the Kochanskis WITHOUT KNOWING their clients' investment objectives, risk tolerance, horizon, and so on; they must make a reasonable inquiry into a client's investment experience, risk and return objectives, and financial constraints PRIOR TO making any investment recommendation; they should have developed an INVESTMENT POLICY STATEMENT for the Kochanskis before they recommended any investments.

Correct. Per BankGlobal Case Solution: 'By developing and recommending a balanced portfolio for the Kochanskis without knowing their clients' investment objectives, risk tolerance, horizon, and so on, Hockett and Lopez violated Standard III(C) Suitability... Hockett and Lopez should have developed an investment policy statement for the Kochanskis before they recommended any investments.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Reasonableness in the abstract cannot substitute for KNOWING the clients' objectives, constraints, and experience before recommending.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The recommendation-before-inquiry sequence is the III(C) violation tested here.

questions.stem: *BankGlobal Case — Lopez presentation of past performance information*: uses average of four funds' five-year annualized returns labeled 'total portfolio return'; shows composite portfolio consisting of team's discretionary accounts of similar size, EXCLUDING terminated accounts. *Most likely* violation caused by:

options.A: Using an average of the four funds' returns to represent the model portfolio's performance was the violation.
options.B: Excluding terminated accounts — Lopez violated Standard III(D) Performance Presentation in her use and presentation of the composite portfolio's performance because she did NOT include the performance of TERMINATED ACCOUNTS.
options.C: Selecting discretionary accounts of similar size to build the composite was the violation.

rationales.A: Incorrect. For this novice audience, an average of the four funds is an acceptable simplification — the audience-tailoring principle.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Excluding terminated accounts — Lopez violated Standard III(D) Performance Presentation in her use and presentation of the composite portfolio's performance because she did NOT include the performance of TERMINATED ACCOUNTS; Lopez should have included terminated AND current discretionary accounts that were similar in her composite.

Correct. Per BankGlobal Case Solution: 'Lopez violated Standard III(D) Performance Presentation in her use and presentation of the composite portfolio's performance because she did not include the performance of terminated accounts. In addition, she included only the team's discretionary accounts of similar size to the Kochanskis'. Lopez should have included terminated and current discretionary accounts that were similar in her composite portfolio's performance. There is no need to include nondiscretionary accounts because clients make their own investment decisions for such accounts.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Similar-size discretionary accounts are an appropriate composite population; the defect is EXCLUDING TERMINATED accounts, inflating the record.

questions.stem: *BankGlobal Case — Hockett generating 'alpha' in conservative accounts*: 90% of client accounts conservative with long-term objectives; generates short-term alpha by investing 15%–20% of portfolios in high-beta stocks that 'appear to have low risk'; no change in IPSs. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: There is no violation because high-beta stocks in modest proportion reduce overall portfolio risk through diversification.
options.B: Hockett's conduct violates only Standard III(D) if the alpha is presented to prospects.
options.C: Standard III(C) Suitability — Hockett is investing in high-beta stocks for client accounts with LONG-TERM OBJECTIVES in order to achieve his short-term quarterly performance bonus.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The 15% high-beta sleeve serves HIS short-term alpha objective, not the accounts' long-term conservative objectives — a suitability breach.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Presentation aside, investing inconsistently with client IPSs violates III(C) directly.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Standard III(C) Suitability — Hockett is investing in high-beta stocks for client accounts with LONG-TERM OBJECTIVES in order to achieve his short-term quarterly performance bonus; per Standard III(C), when responsible for managing a portfolio to a specific mandate, strategy, or style, must make only investment recommendations or take only investment actions that are CONSISTENT with the stated objectives and constraints; high-beta stocks by their very nature HAVE RISK; the high-beta stocks are contrary to the long-term risk profile established in the clients' IPSs; Hockett changed the investment strategy in an attempt to gain short-term rewards from firm's new compensation arrangement and not in response to changes in investment needs.

Correct. Per BankGlobal Case Solution: 'Hockett is violating Standard III(C) Suitability by investing in high-beta stocks for client accounts with long-term objectives in order to achieve his short-term quarterly performance bonus... High-beta stocks by their very nature have risk. Hockett is also violating Standard III(C) by investing 15%-20% of his clients' portfolios in high-beta stocks that "appear low risk." These high-beta stocks are contrary to the long-term risk profile established in the clients' investment policy statements (IPSs). Hockett changed the investment strategy for his clients in an attempt to reap short-term rewards offered by his firm's new compensation arrangement and not in response to changes in their investment needs.'

questions.stem: *BankGlobal Case — Lopez social media post*: after work Lopez posts to old high school friends: 'Just opened an account for Mr. Kochanski and his wife.' *Most likely* violates:

options.A: Standard III(E) Preservation of Confidentiality — members must keep information about current, former, and prospective clients confidential unless: (1) info concerns illegal activities.
options.B: There is no violation because the post revealed no investment details or account balances.
options.C: There is no violation because the audience was limited to old high school friends rather than the public.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Standard III(E) Preservation of Confidentiality — members must keep information about current, former, and prospective clients confidential unless: (1) info concerns illegal activities; (2) disclosure is required by law; or (3) the client permits disclosure; Standard III(E) requires that EVEN THE PRESENCE OF A CLIENT ACCOUNT be kept confidentially within the firm unless the client has been informed and has consented to the disclosure of the information; confidentiality extends BEYOND the investment industry to the population as a whole.

Correct. Per BankGlobal Case Solution: 'Lopez violated Standard III(E) Preservation of Confidentiality by posting about the Kochanskis' opening of an account... Standard III(E) requires that even the presence of a client account be kept confidentially within the firm unless the client has been informed and has consented to the disclosure of the information.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The EXISTENCE of the client relationship is itself confidential information under III(E).
rationales.C: Incorrect. Audience size does not matter; disclosure to anyone outside authorized colleagues breaches confidentiality.

questions.stem: *Castle Bio 1 — Plume not disclosing Global's ownership by Castle*: Plume writes research reports on STRX/APBX (subsidiaries of Castle Biotechnology) but does not disclose in his research reports that Global (his employer) is owned by Castle. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: There is no violation because the ownership relationship is publicly disclosed in the subsidiaries' prospectuses.
options.B: Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts — by not disclosing that Global is owned by Castle in his research report, Plume violates VI(A).
options.C: The failure is one of independence under Standard I(B), since Castle's ownership pressures Plume's conclusions.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Public availability elsewhere does not satisfy VI(A) — the conflict must be disclosed IN THE RESEARCH REPORT itself.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts — by not disclosing that Global is owned by Castle in his research report, Plume violates VI(A); CB's ownership of Global and Plume's close relationships with CB's management raise questions about Plume's ability to remain independent and objective; the fact that relationships are disclosed in offering prospectuses is NECESSARY BUT NOT SUFFICIENT to meet Standard VI(A) because they may not communicate the information effectively or in a prominent manner.

Correct. Per Castle Bio 1 Solution: 'By not disclosing that Global is owned by Castle in his research report, Plume is in violation of Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts... CB's ownership of Global and Plume's close relationships with CB's management raise questions about Plume's ability to remain independent and objective... The fact that the relationships between Plume, CB, its subsidiaries, and Global are disclosed in the offering prospectus is necessary but not sufficient to meet Standard VI(A) because they may not communicate the information effectively or in a prominent manner.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Pressure may exist, but the tested defect is the missing conflict disclosure in his reports.

questions.stem: *Castle Bio 1 — Plume not disclosing his ownership of CB Class A preferred shares and CB common stock options*: Plume owns CB preferred shares and options granted while employee; does not own STRX/APBX shares directly. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: There is no violation because Plume owns no shares of STRX or APBX, the companies he covers.
options.B: There is no violation because preferred shares and options confer no beneficial interest in the subsidiaries.
options.C: Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts — Plume's ownership of CB's preferred shares and stock options, along with CB's voting control of each company, gives him a BENEFICIAL OWNERSHIP INTEREST in STRX and APBX.

rationales.A: Incorrect. His CB securities plus CB's voting control of the subsidiaries create an INDIRECT beneficial interest in the covered companies.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The indirect pecuniary link through the parent is precisely what VI(A) requires him to disclose.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts — Plume's ownership of CB's preferred shares and stock options, along with CB's voting control of each company, gives him a BENEFICIAL OWNERSHIP INTEREST in STRX and APBX; for the purposes of Standard VI(A), members and candidates BENEFICIALLY OWN securities if they have a DIRECT OR INDIRECT PECUNIARY INTEREST, have the POWER TO DIRECT VOTING, or have the POWER TO DISPOSE.

Correct. Per Castle Bio 1 Solution: 'By not disclosing his ownership of CB's preferred shares and stock options in his research report, Plume is in violation of Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts. Because of this ownership in CB, Plume has a beneficial ownership interest in the securities of STRX and APBX, both of which he recommended as "buys." The most obvious conflict that must always be disclosed involves relationships between an issuer and the member, the candidate, or his or her firm. For the purposes of Standard VI(A), members and candidates beneficially own securities or other investments if they have a direct or indirect pecuniary interest in the securities, have the power to direct the voting of the shares of the securities or investments, or have the power to dispose of or direct the disposition of the security or investment.'

questions.curriculum_ref: V5 LM9 — Castle Bio 1 (Plume ownership CB)

questions.stem: *Castle Bio 1 — Plume's annual bonus based on IPO gross proceeds*: bonus equal to 0.10% of gross proceeds for each IPO he works on with corporate finance department; not disclosed in his research report. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: Standard I(B) Independence and Objectivity — members must use reasonable care and judgment to achieve and maintain independence and objectivity.
options.B: There is no violation because bonus arrangements tied to firm revenues are standard sell-side compensation.
options.C: The arrangement implicates only Standard VI(A) and is cured by disclosure in the research reports.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Standard I(B) Independence and Objectivity — members must use reasonable care and judgment to achieve and maintain independence and objectivity; must NOT accept any gift, benefit, compensation, or consideration that reasonably could be expected to COMPROMISE independence and objectivity; compensation arrangements should NOT LINK analyst remuneration DIRECTLY to investment banking assignments in which the analysts may participate as a team member; Global can avoid the conflict by REMOVING this incentive from Plume's compensation package.

Correct. Per Castle Bio 1 Solution: 'By not disclosing his annual bonus in his research report, Plume is in violation of Standard I(B) Independence and Objectivity... The guidance for Standard I(B) states, "Compensation arrangements should not link analyst remuneration directly to investment banking assignments in which the analysts may participate as a team member." The guidance also states, "Having analysts work with investment bankers is appropriate only when the conflicts are adequately and effectively managed and disclosed... Firms should require public disclosure of actual conflicts of interest to investors." Global can avoid the conflict by removing this incentive from Plume's compensation package.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. A bonus tied to IPO proceeds he works on gives Plume a direct stake in banking outcomes — compromising research independence under I(B).
rationales.C: Incorrect. Disclosure helps, but compensation structurally linked to banking success attacks independence itself — I(B) is the operative standard.

questions.stem: *Castle Bio 1 — Plume selling APBX short in personal portfolio*: three months after issuing 'buy' rec on APBX, Plume sells APBX short in personal portfolio; does not disclose because he was 'never a beneficial owner of APBX.' *Most likely* violates:

options.A: There is no violation because analysts may manage personal portfolios according to their own risk views.
options.B: Standard VI(A) — selling APBX short in his personal portfolio while having a 'buy' recommendation on the stock is a conflict of interest that Plume needs to disclose.
options.C: There is no violation because a short position is not beneficial ownership of the security.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Holding a personal SHORT against his own outstanding BUY recommendation is a conflict that must be disclosed — personal risk views do not erase it.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Standard VI(A) — selling APBX short in his personal portfolio while having a 'buy' recommendation on the stock is a conflict of interest that Plume needs to disclose; Global could avoid this conflict by PROHIBITING such transactions by its employees; Plume's ownership of CB's preferred shares and common stock options, along with CB's voting control, gives him a beneficial ownership interest in APBX.

Correct. Per Castle Bio 1 Solution: 'By selling APBX short in his personal portfolio, Plume is in violation of Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts... Selling APBX short in his personal portfolio while having a "buy" recommendation on the stock is a conflict of interest that Plume needs to disclose. Global could avoid this conflict by prohibiting such transactions by its employees.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. A short position is a direct pecuniary interest in the covered security's decline; the conflict is real and disclosable.

questions.stem: *Castle Bio 2 — Benning using social media/email to solicit former Kodiak clients after leaving*: uses personal social media platforms and email to contact clients to inform them of her departure and solicit them to follow her to Global AFTER she resigned. *Most likely*:

options.A: violates IV(A) — duty of loyalty.
options.B: violates III(E) — confidentiality.
options.C: NO violation — Benning's solicitation of former clients via social media does not violate the Code and Standards.

rationales.A: No violation — post-departure OK.
rationales.B: Post-departure allowed.
rationales.C:
Full statement: NO violation — Benning's solicitation of former clients via social media does not violate the Code and Standards; per guidance to Standard IV(A) Loyalty, once employment with the former firm has ended, the standard does NOT prohibit former employees from CONTACTING CLIENTS of their previous firm as long as the contact information does not come from the records of or as a result of work for their former employer or such outreach does not violate an applicable agreement with the former firm.

Correct. Per Castle Bio 2 Solution: 'Benning's solicitation of former clients via social media does not violate the Code and Standards. The guidance to Standard IV(A) Loyalty states that once employment with the former firm has ended, the standard does not prohibit former employees from contacting clients of their previous firm as long as the contact information does not come from the records of as a result of work for their former employer or such outreach does not violate an applicable agreement with the former firm. Benning's employment with Kodak ended once she resigned, left the firm and no longer has responsibilities or is being paid by the firm.'

questions.stem: *Castle Bio 2 — Benning's $250,000 signing bonus based on percentage of Kodiak clients who moved to Global*: does not disclose to clients. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts — Benning's decision to solicit her Kodiak clients to move to Global could have been INFLUENCED by the fact that her signing bonus from Global was based on the percentage of her Kodiak clients who moved their accounts.
options.B: There is no violation because a signing bonus is employment compensation rather than a referral fee.
options.C: The arrangement violates Standard VI(C) as an undisclosed referral fee.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts — Benning's decision to solicit her Kodiak clients to move to Global could have been INFLUENCED by the fact that her signing bonus from Global was based on the percentage of her Kodiak clients who moved their accounts; this creates an ACTUAL OR POTENTIAL CONFLICT of interest that she must disclose to her former Kodiak clients; the bonus is compensation paid by employer to employee (not a referral fee under VI(C)), but the potential impairment of independence and objectivity triggers VI(A) disclosure.

Correct. Per Castle Bio 2 Solution: 'By not disclosing to her Kodiak clients the signing bonus she received from Global, Benning is in violation of Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts. Benning's decision to solicit her Kodiak clients to move to Global could have been influenced by the fact that her signing bonus from Global was based on the percentage of her Kodiak clients who moved their accounts to Global. This creates an actual or potential conflict of interest that she must disclose to her former Kodiak clients.' Also: 'It is true that the bonus paid by Global represents compensation paid by the employer to the employee for services provided and is not a referral fee.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Correct that it is not a referral fee — but a bonus contingent on clients MOVING with her could have influenced her solicitation, a VI(A) conflict requiring disclosure.
rationales.C: Incorrect. No third-party referral exists; the defect is the undisclosed conflict of interest, not VI(C).

questions.curriculum_ref: V5 LM9 — Castle Bio 2 (Benning signing bonus)

questions.stem: *Castle Bio 2 — Benning's year-end bonus based on level of clients' IPO participation*: not disclosed to clients. *Most likely*:

options.A: no violation.
options.B: Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts — year-end bonus is based on the level of her clients' participation in IPOs.
options.C: Standard IV(B) additional compensation.

rationales.A: VI(A) — client-participation bonus is conflict.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts — year-end bonus is based on the level of her clients' participation in IPOs; creates a conflict of interest between Benning and her clients; is she recommending IPOs to clients based on their best interest or hers? Benning is in violation of VI(A); Global can avoid this conflict by not compensating Benning in this way.

Correct. Per Castle Bio 2 Solution: 'Benning's year-end bonus is based on the level of her clients' participation in IPOs. This situation creates a conflict of interest between Benning and her clients. Is she recommending IPOs to clients based on their best interest or hers? Benning is in violation of Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose of Conflicts... Global can avoid this conflict by not compensating Benning in this way.'
rationales.C: VI(A) year-end conflict.

questions.stem: *Castle Bio 2 — FTSX tie-in agreement*: Benning allocates IPO shares to institutional clients who plan on purchasing additional shares in the secondary market; intended to artificially increase demand and support higher share price. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: There is no violation because Benning followed her employer's allocation policy for the offering.
options.B: There is no violation because allocating to clients who want more shares treats interested clients fairly.
options.C: Standard II(B) Market Manipulation AND Standard III(B) Fair Dealing — this is a TIE-IN AGREEMENT, where the underwriter requires the investor to buy more shares of a new issue in the secondary market as a condition of buying shares in the IPO.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Employer policy cannot authorize a tie-in scheme designed to inflate aftermarket demand.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Conditioning IPO allocation on secondary-market buying manipulates the market (II(B)) and corrupts fair allocation (III(B)).
rationales.C:
Full statement: Standard II(B) Market Manipulation AND Standard III(B) Fair Dealing — this is a TIE-IN AGREEMENT, where the underwriter requires the investor to buy more shares of a new issue in the secondary market as a condition of buying shares in the IPO; intended to artificially increase demand and support a higher share price; tie-in agreements are a violation of II(B) which states 'must not engage in practices that distort prices or artificially inflate trading volume with the intent to mislead market participants'; also violates III(B) Fair Dealing because Benning is not treating all her institutional clients fairly — allocating IPO shares only to those who agree to purchase additional shares.

Correct. Per Castle Bio 2 Solution: 'This is an example of a tie-in agreement, where the underwriter requires the investor to buy more shares of a new issue in the secondary market as a condition of buying shares in the IPO. It is intended to artificially increase demand and support a higher share price. Tie-in agreements are a violation of Standard II(B) Market Manipulation... This practice also violates Standard III(B) Fair Dealing... In this case, Benning is not treating all her institutional clients fairly, because she is allocating IPO shares only to those clients who agree to purchase additional shares after FTSX begins trading on the first day.'

questions.stem: *Castle Bio 2 — Benning initialing Option Account form on Deacon's behalf*: after Deacon signed, Benning changed language pertaining to Deacon's suitability for options; to save Deacon another trip, Benning initials form on Deacon's behalf. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: Standard I(D) Misconduct — members must NOT engage in any professional conduct involving dishonesty, fraud, or deceit or commit any act that reflects adversely on their professional reputation, integrity, or competence.
options.B: There is no violation because Benning acted to save Deacon time and with her implicit blessing.
options.C: The conduct is unaddressed by the Standards because account paperwork is administrative.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Standard I(D) Misconduct — members must NOT engage in any professional conduct involving dishonesty, fraud, or deceit or commit any act that reflects adversely on their professional reputation, integrity, or competence; by changing the language on the Option Account Application and Agreement and initialing the form on behalf of Deacon, Benning violates I(D); even if acting in Deacon's best interests, changes cannot be effected without Deacon's own written approval.

Correct. Per Castle Bio 2 Solution: 'By changing the language on the Option Account Application and Agreement and initialing the form on behalf of Deacon, Benning is in violation of Standard I(D) Misconduct... According to Standard I(D) Misconduct, "Members and Candidates must not engage in any professional conduct involving dishonesty, fraud, or deceit or commit any act that reflects adversely on their professional reputation, integrity, or competence."'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Altering a signed suitability document and initialing on the client's behalf is dishonesty and deceit — client convenience is no defense.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Fraudulent document handling is professional misconduct squarely under I(D).

questions.stem: *Castle Bio 2 — Benning signing Account Linking form on Deacon's behalf after phone approval*: obtains Deacon's phone authorization but signs the form herself. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: no violation.
options.B: Standard I(D) Misconduct — signing the Account Linking form on behalf of Deacon is also a violation of I(D).
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: I(D) — phone approval insufficient.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Standard I(D) Misconduct — signing the Account Linking form on behalf of Deacon is also a violation of I(D); the approval Benning received over the telephone to sign the Account Linking form is INSUFFICIENT; Benning needs Deacon to PERSONALLY sign the authorization.

Correct. Per Castle Bio 2 Solution: 'Signing the Account Linking form on behalf of Deacon is also a violation of Standard I(D). The approval Benning received over the telephone to sign the Account Linking form is insufficient. Benning needs Deacon to personally sign the authorization.'
rationales.C: Phone insufficient.

questions.stem: *Castle Bio 2 — Benning transacting at Steve Deacon's request*: Steve (husband) informs Benning wife has serious health issues, requests $15,000 redemption; Benning completes several redemptions and withdrawals at Steve's request over next few weeks. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: There is no violation because a family medical emergency justified prompt action on the household's behalf.
options.B: The Standards do not address spousal instructions on individually held accounts.
options.C: Standard III(A) Loyalty, Prudence, and Care — Benning has no authority or authorization from Deacon, the account holder, allowing Deacon's husband Steve to redeem shares and approve withdrawals from her account.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Sympathy does not create authority — the account holder gave no authorization for her husband to direct transactions.
rationales.B: Incorrect. III(A) governs directly: acting on an unauthorized third party's instructions breaches the duty to the client.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Standard III(A) Loyalty, Prudence, and Care — Benning has no authority or authorization from Deacon, the account holder, allowing Deacon's husband Steve to redeem shares and approve withdrawals from her account; even if Benning was acting in Deacon's best interests, transacting at husband's request without Deacon's authorization violates the Standards.

Correct. Per Castle Bio 2 Solution: 'By redeeming shares and transacting (withdrawals) in Deacon's account at her husband's request, Benning is in violation of Standard III(A) Loyalty, Prudence, and Care. Benning has no authority or authorization from Deacon, the account holder, allowing Deacon's husband, Steve, to redeem shares and approve withdrawals from her account.'

questions.stem: *Lionsgate 1 — Hill's marketing statement of VCF performance gross of fees*: 'VCF is best-performing fund in Australian equities over 10-year period. VCF delivered returns of 28.7%, gross of fees, over 1-year period.' *Most likely*:

options.A: NO violation — Hill states the performance is gross of fees or 'pre-fees'.
options.B: violates III(D) — gross of fees.
options.C: violates I(C).

rationales.A:
Full statement: NO violation — Hill states the performance is gross of fees or 'pre-fees'; per Standard III(D) Performance Presentation guidance, 'Members and Candidates can meet their obligations under Standard III(D) by including disclosures that fully explain the performance results being reported (for example... disclosing whether the performance is gross of fees, net of fees, or after tax).'

Correct. Per Lionsgate 1 Solution: 'There is no evidence to suggest Hill's statements are false or opinion based. In speaking about the fund's performance, Hill is stating facts. In addition, Hill states the performance is gross of fees or "pre-fees." According to Standard III(D) Performance Presentation... Members and Candidates can meet their obligations under Standard III(D) by "including disclosures that fully explain the performance results being reported (for example, stating, when appropriate, that results are simulated when model results are used, clearly indicating when the performance record is that of a prior entity, or disclosing whether the performance is gross of fees, net of fees, or after tax)."'
rationales.B: Gross of fees disclosed = OK.
rationales.C: III(D) disclosure requirement met.

questions.stem: *Lionsgate 1 — Hill accepting 'thank you' gift bags from talk show sponsors*: contains wine, retail gift cards, restaurant certificates, travel discounts; does not disclose to LL. *Most likely*:

options.A: Accepting the gift bags violates Standard IV(B) because Hill did not obtain written consent from all parties.
options.B: NO violation — per Standard IV(B) Additional Compensation Arrangements, must not accept gifts that competes with or might reasonably be expected to create a conflict of interest with EMPLOYER'S INTEREST unless obtain written consent.
options.C: Accepting the gift bags violates Standard I(B) by compromising Hill's independence and objectivity.

rationales.A: Incorrect. IV(B) is triggered by benefits that conflict with the EMPLOYER'S interest — ordinary speaker gift bags from show sponsors create no such conflict.
rationales.B:
Full statement: NO violation — per Standard IV(B) Additional Compensation Arrangements, must not accept gifts that competes with or might reasonably be expected to create a conflict of interest with EMPLOYER'S INTEREST unless obtain written consent; Hill's appearance on shows does NOT create a conflict of interest with the interests of LL or the VCF.

Correct. Per Lionsgate 1 Solution: 'According to Standard IV(B) Additional Compensation Arrangements, "Members and Candidates must not accept gifts, benefits, compensation, or consideration that competes with or might reasonably be expected to create a conflict of interest with their employer's interest unless they obtain written consent from all parties involved." Hill's appearance on these shows does not create a conflict of interest with the interests of LL or the VCF.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Nothing about wine and gift cards from talk-show sponsors reasonably compromises his investment independence.

questions.stem: *Lionsgate 1 — Hill and 10 team members planning new firm outside work hours*: use time outside of work and on weekends to lease office space and register with government authorities. *Most likely*:

options.A: violates IV(A) duty of loyalty.
options.B: violates III(A).
options.C: NO violation — Hill and individuals joining him are making preparations OUTSIDE of work and on weekends.

rationales.A: Off-hours preparation OK.
rationales.B: No violation off-hours.
rationales.C:
Full statement: NO violation — Hill and individuals joining him are making preparations OUTSIDE of work and on weekends; their actions to start a new business do NOT occur during hours when their employer would expect them to be at work; thus, they are not in violation of the standard.

Correct. Per Lionsgate 1 Solution: 'Hill and the individuals joining him at the new firm are using their time outside of work and on weekends to lease office space and to individually register with government authorities. Because they are not using work time, they are not breaching their duty of loyalty to Lionsgate.'

questions.stem: *Lionsgate 1 — Hill telling team to solicit former clients on new firm's opening day*: since there is no noncompete agreement, they can begin soliciting former clients the day they open new firm. *Most likely*:

options.A: NO violation — per guidance to Standard IV(A), once employment with the former firm has ended, the standard does not prohibit members and candidates of their previous firm as long as the contact information does not come from the records of or as a result of work for their former employer or such outreach does not violate an applicable agreement.
options.B: The instruction violates Standard IV(A) because departing employees remain barred from soliciting their former firm's clients for one full year after termination, regardless of whether a noncompete agreement exists.
options.C: The instruction violates Standard IV(C) because Hill, as the new firm's leader, failed to establish supervisory procedures governing how his team conducts solicitation of former clients.

rationales.A:
Full statement: NO violation — per guidance to Standard IV(A), once employment with the former firm has ended, the standard does not prohibit members and candidates of their previous firm as long as the contact information does not come from the records of or as a result of work for their former employer or such outreach does not violate an applicable agreement; by telling his team to begin solicitation AFTER they have left the VCF and on the new firm's opening day — not prior to its opening — and since there is no noncompete agreement, there is no violation.

Correct. Per Lionsgate 1 Solution: 'Hill is not in violation of the Code and Standards... By telling his team to begin solicitation after they have left the VCF and on the new firm's opening day — not prior to its opening — and since there is no noncompete agreement, there is no violation of Standard IV(A).'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Absent a noncompete or use of former-firm records, post-departure solicitation is permitted — no cooling-off period exists in the Code.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Directing lawful post-departure solicitation is not a supervision failure.

questions.stem: *Lionsgate 2 — Portman describing Martin as Hill's 'assistant'*: invites Hill to important client meetings; discusses Hill's stock-picking acumen; refers questions to Martin (whom he calls Hill's 'assistant') even though Martin has actually been making investment decisions. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: There is no violation because presenting senior colleagues favorably is ordinary sales practice.
options.B: Standard I(C) Misrepresentation — by inviting Hill to client meetings and referring to Martin as Hill's 'assistant,' Portman is giving clients the IMPRESSION that Hill is responsible for stock selection and investment decision making at the fund.
options.C: The violation is Standard I(C), but for misrepresenting the fund's investment strategy rather than personnel roles.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Creating the impression that Hill manages the money while the actual manager is cast as his 'assistant' misrepresents who does what — beyond puffery.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Standard I(C) Misrepresentation — by inviting Hill to client meetings and referring to Martin as Hill's 'assistant,' Portman is giving clients the IMPRESSION that Hill is responsible for stock selection and investment decision making at the fund; Portman is in violation of I(C) — 'Members and Candidates must not knowingly make any misrepresentations relating to investment analysis, recommendations, actions, or other professional activities.'

Correct. Per Lionsgate 2 Solution: 'By inviting Hill to client meetings and referring to Martin as Hill's "assistant," Portman is giving clients the impression that Hill is responsible for stock selection and investment decision making at the fund. Portman is in violation of Standard I(C) Misrepresentation: "Members and Candidates must not knowingly make any misrepresentations relating to investment analysis, recommendations, actions, or other professional activities."'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The misrepresentation concerns ROLES and responsibilities, not the strategy.

questions.stem: *Lionsgate 2 — CIO and 2 board members selling shares in LL and VCF*: sold shares BEFORE Hill's resignation was announced publicly. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: There is no violation because insiders may trade their own company's stock at any time.
options.B: There is no violation because a portfolio manager's resignation is never material information.
options.C: Standard II(A) Material Nonpublic Information — by selling their shares before Hill's resignation was announced, they acted on material nonpublic information.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Insider status heightens rather than removes the MNPI prohibition — they sold on undisclosed information.
rationales.B: Incorrect. A star manager's departure is plainly material to LL and VCF; selling before the announcement is trading on MNPI.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Standard II(A) Material Nonpublic Information — by selling their shares before Hill's resignation was announced, they acted on material nonpublic information; per II(A), 'Members and Candidates who possess material nonpublic information that could affect the value of an investment must not act or cause others to act on the information'; information is MATERIAL if its disclosure would probably have an impact on the price of a security.

Correct. Per Lionsgate 2 Solution: 'By selling their shares before Hill's resignation was announced, they acted on material nonpublic information. According to Standard II(A) Material Nonpublic Information, "Members and Candidates who possess material nonpublic information that could affect the value of an investment must not act or cause others to act on the information." Information is "material" if its disclosure would probably have an impact on the price of a security or if reasonable investors would want to know the information before making an investment decision.'

questions.stem: *Lionsgate 2 — Portman selling his shares AFTER seeing Sky News report that CIO and 2 board members sold*: does not know definitively whether Hill is resigning; learned of officer sales from public news. *Most likely*:

options.A: NO violation — Portman does not know definitively whether Hill is resigning.
options.B: violates IV(A).
options.C: violates II(A).

rationales.A:
Full statement: NO violation — Portman does not know definitively whether Hill is resigning; his sell decision is based on learning from Sky News Business Channel that CIO and two board members sold their shares; Portman's decision to sell is NOT based on material nonpublic information because this information was PUBLICLY REPORTED; he is not in violation of II(A).

Correct. Per Lionsgate 2 Solution: 'In this case, Portman does not know definitively whether Hill is resigning. In addition to the rumors he heard, Portman's sell decision is based on learning from Sky News Business Channel that the CIO and two board members sold their shares. Portman's decision to sell is not based on material nonpublic information, because this information was publicly reported. He is not in violation of Standard II(A) Material Nonpublic Information.'
rationales.B: Publicly-reported info OK to act on.
rationales.C: Public info = no violation.

questions.stem: *Graeme Case — disclosing new issue commissions only to clients who ask*: BOA's policy does not require disclosure since clients receive prospectuses; Graeme discloses only to clients who ask. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: no violation — BOA policy.
options.B: Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts — Graeme can avoid the conflict by NOT ACCEPTING commissions.
options.C: no violation — clients receive prospectuses.

rationales.A: VI(A) — all clients not just askers.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts — Graeme can avoid the conflict by NOT ACCEPTING commissions; if he does not avoid the conflict, Graeme must inform ALL CLIENTS, not just those who ask, of the commissions he receives on new issues so that his clients have the information necessary to evaluate the objectivity of the investment advice; VI(A) requires this regardless of BOA's policy and regardless of what appears in prospectuses.

Correct. Per Graeme Case Solution: 'Graeme is in violation of Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts... Graeme can avoid the conflict by not accepting commissions from new issues. If he does not avoid the conflict, Graeme must inform all clients, not just those who ask, of the commissions he receives on new issues so that his clients have the information necessary to evaluate the objectivity of the investment advice or action taken by him on their behalf.'
rationales.C: Prospectus insufficient.

questions.stem: *Delaneys — Graeme purchasing new issues held less than 5 months for account with 15-20yr horizon, low risk tolerance*: majority of Delaneys' purchases were new issues; longest hold 5 months. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: no violation.
options.B: Standard III(D).
options.C: Standard III(C) Suitability — Delaneys' risk tolerance is LOW.

rationales.A: III(C) — low risk tolerance breached.
rationales.B: Suitability.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Standard III(C) Suitability — Delaneys' risk tolerance is LOW; they were unwilling to accept high levels of volatility; by purchasing new issues in their account and holding them for less than 5 months, Graeme's actions are unsuitable for Delaneys' risk tolerance; must determine that an investment is suitable to the client's financial situation and consistent with client's written objectives, mandates, and constraints.

Correct. Per Delaneys Solution: 'The Delaneys stated that their risk tolerance is low, which means they were unwilling to accept high levels of volatility in their portfolio. By purchasing new issues in their account and holding them for less than five months, Graeme's actions are unsuitable for the Delaneys' risk tolerance... Graeme violated Standard III(C) Suitability.'

questions.stem: *Balmer's investigation of Graeme's new issue use*: Balmer raised concerns during meeting; escalated by reporting to BOA's director of compliance. *Most likely*:

options.A: NO violation — Balmer followed the Code and Standards.
options.B: violates IV(C).
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: NO violation — Balmer followed the Code and Standards; she raised concerns with Graeme and escalated to the director of compliance; per IV(C) Responsibilities of Supervisors, must make reasonable efforts to ensure that anyone subject to their supervision or authority complies with laws, rules, regulations, and the C&S; both raising concerns and escalating are consistent with supervisor responsibilities.

Correct. Per Delaneys Solution: 'Balmer did not violate the Code and Standards. She raised concerns to Graeme during her end of the year meeting and then escalated her concerns by reporting them to BOA's director of compliance. According to Standard IV(C) Responsibilities of Supervisors... Balmer did what is required of a supervisor.'
rationales.B: Balmer's actions comply.
rationales.C: IV(C) compliance.

questions.stem: *Milgram case — Graeme not updating Milgram's IPS since inception (5 years)*: Milgram's IPS has never been updated since it was first put in place five years ago. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: no violation.
options.B: Standard III(C) Suitability — must reassess and update this information REGULARLY.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: III(C) — no updates.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Standard III(C) Suitability — must reassess and update this information REGULARLY; Graeme has not updated Milgram's IPS since it was first put in place, which places him in violation of III(C).

Correct. Per Milgram Solution: 'Graeme has not updated Milgram's IPS since it was first put in place, which places him in violation of Standard III(C).'
rationales.C: III(C) updates.

questions.stem: *Balmer's handling of Milgram's complaint*: called Graeme into office; sat in on meeting where Graeme reviewed Milgram's account and IPS. *Most likely*:

options.A: Balmer violated Standard IV(C) by failing to escalate Milgram's complaint to the board immediately.
options.B: The Standards do not address how supervisors respond to client complaints.
options.C: NO violation — as soon as she was made aware of the complaint, Balmer initiated an INVESTIGATION by calling Graeme into her office.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Her prompt investigation — calling Graeme in and reviewing the account and IPS — is exactly the reasonable supervisory response; board escalation is not the first step.
rationales.B: Incorrect. IV(C) governs the supervisory response, and Balmer's met it.
rationales.C:
Full statement: NO violation — as soon as she was made aware of the complaint, Balmer initiated an INVESTIGATION by calling Graeme into her office; she sat in on the meeting where Graeme reviewed Milgram's account and IPS; per IV(C), once a supervisor learns that an employee has violated or may have violated the C&S, the supervisor must promptly INITIATE AN ASSESSMENT to determine the extent of the wrongdoing; relying on employee's statements or assurances is NOT enough — Balmer's active investigation meets IV(C).

Correct. Per Milgram Solution: 'Balmer did not violate the Code and Standards. As soon as she was made aware of the complaint, she initiated an investigation by calling Graeme into her office. She sat in on the meeting, where Graeme reviewed Milgram's account and IPS. According to Standard IV(C) Responsibilities of Supervisors... once a supervisor learns that an employee has violated or may have violated the law or the Code and Standards, the supervisor must promptly initiate an assessment to determine the extent of the wrongdoing. Relying on an employee's statements about the extent of the violation or assurances that the wrongdoing will not reoccur is not enough.'

questions.stem: *GHB Case — Yousoff not disclosing fee-sharing arrangement in MOU*: Yousoff/Wong decide to pay Tan and Chuong (board members who built relationship with MGM2) a portion of MGM2's advisory fees; not disclosed in the MOU. *Most likely*:

options.A: NO violation — the fee-sharing arrangement is a REWARD paid to Tan and Chuong for their efforts to bring MGM2 on as a client.
options.B: The arrangement violates Standard VI(C) as an undisclosed referral fee paid to Tan and Chuong.
options.C: The arrangement violates Standard VI(A) as an unmanaged conflict of interest within the MOU.

rationales.A:
Full statement: NO violation — the fee-sharing arrangement is a REWARD paid to Tan and Chuong for their efforts to bring MGM2 on as a client; the fee arrangement is NOT a referral fee; Tan and Chuong did not refer MGM2 to GHB; a referral fee is compensation for the RECOMMENDATION of products or services; case facts provide no evidence that a conflict of interest is present.

Correct. Per GHB Case Solution: 'Yousoff is not in violation of the Code and Standards. The fee-sharing arrangement is a reward paid to Tan and Chuong for their efforts to bring MGM2 on as a client. The fee arrangement is not a referral fee. Tan and Chuong did not refer MGM2 to GHB. A referral fee is compensation, consideration, or benefit received from or paid to others for the recommendation of products or services.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The payment rewards board members' business-development efforts on a won mandate — compensation for their work, not a client-referral fee.
rationales.C: Incorrect. As structured, the reward arrangement is legitimate compensation; no violation arises from the MOU itself.

questions.stem: *GHB Case — Chuong not disclosing information about her son to Yousoff*: Hie (who helped found MGM2) recently hired Chuong's son in executive role. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: There is no violation because a son's employment is his own affair, not his mother's disclosure obligation.
options.B: Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts — Hie's hiring of Chuong's son in an executive position at Hie's firm could reasonably be expected to IMPAIR Chuong's independence and objectivity with respect to interactions between MGM2 and GHB.
options.C: The failure is Standard I(C), because staying silent misrepresents Chuong's relationships to the firm.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Hie hiring her son into an executive role could reasonably impair Chuong's objectivity toward Hie's business — a disclosable VI(A) conflict.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts — Hie's hiring of Chuong's son in an executive position at Hie's firm could reasonably be expected to IMPAIR Chuong's independence and objectivity with respect to interactions between MGM2 and GHB; VI(A) requires Chuong to disclose this conflict.

Correct. Per GHB Case Solution: 'Hie's hiring of Chuong's son in an executive position at Hie's firm could reasonably be expected to impair Chuong's independence and objectivity with respect to interactions between MGM2 and GHB. Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts requires Chuong to disclose this conflict.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Silence here is an undisclosed CONFLICT, not an affirmative misrepresentation.

questions.stem: *GHB Case — Sim destroying documentation of meeting with Hie at his request*: uses fact sheet Hie provided to prepare IPS; destroys other notes she took during meeting. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: There is no violation because destroying the notes honored the client's confidentiality request.
options.B: The Standards do not govern an adviser's personal meeting notes.
options.C: Standard V(C) Record Retention — members must develop and maintain APPROPRIATE RECORDS to support their investment analyses, recommendations, actions, and other investment-related communications.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Confidentiality protects information from outsiders; it does not authorize destroying records that support the IPS and advice.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Meeting notes underpinning recommendations are exactly the records V(C) requires be developed and maintained.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Standard V(C) Record Retention — members must develop and maintain APPROPRIATE RECORDS to support their investment analyses, recommendations, actions, and other investment-related communications; some examples of supporting documentation include notes from clients; by destroying all notes from their meeting, Sim is not maintaining appropriate records to support her investment actions.

Correct. Per GHB Case Solution: 'Sim violated Standard V(C) Record Retention: "Members and Candidates must develop and maintain appropriate records to support their investment analyses, recommendations, actions, and other investment-related communications with clients and prospective clients." The guidance for Standard V(C) states, "Members and candidates must retain records that substantiate the scope of their research and reasons for their actions or conclusions." Some examples of supporting documentation include notes from clients. By destroying all notes from their meeting, Sim is not maintaining appropriate records to support her investment actions.'

questions.stem: *GHB Case — Sim not disclosing Hie's promised year-end bonus to Yousoff*: Hie promises Sim a bonus at year-end if she processes transactions in a timely manner. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: Standard IV(B) Additional Compensation Arrangements — members must NOT accept gifts, benefits, compensation, or consideration that competes with or might reasonably be expected to create a conflict of interest with their employer's interest UNLESS they obtain written consent from all parties involved.
options.B: There is no violation because a bonus offered by a grateful client is a personal gift rather than compensation, and gifts from clients fall entirely outside the scope of the additional-compensation standard.
options.C: The promised bonus violates Standard VI(A) as an undisclosed conflict of interest that Sim was required to disclose prominently to all of her other clients.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Standard IV(B) Additional Compensation Arrangements — members must NOT accept gifts, benefits, compensation, or consideration that competes with or might reasonably be expected to create a conflict of interest with their employer's interest UNLESS they obtain written consent from all parties involved; Hie's promise of a bonus if Sim processes his transactions in a timely manner could conflict with Sim's responsibilities to her other clients or other clients of GHB; at a minimum, Sim must disclose the bonus arrangement to Yousoff, her employer.

Correct. Per GHB Case Solution: 'By not disclosing her bonus arrangement with Hie, Sim violated the Code and Standards. According to Standard IV(B) Additional Compensation Arrangements, "Members and Candidates must not accept gifts, benefits, compensation, or consideration that competes with or might reasonably be expected to create a conflict of interest with their employer's interest unless they obtain written consent from all parties involved." Hie's promise of a bonus if Sim processes his transactions in a timely manner could conflict with Sim's responsibilities to her other clients or other clients of GHB.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. A promised year-end bonus contingent on service creates an arrangement that can conflict with the employer's interest — IV(B) requires written consent before acceptance.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The operative failure is the undisclosed additional-compensation arrangement with the employer, governed by IV(B).

questions.stem: *GHB Case — Sim opening Hie's account without full inquiry*: Hie refuses to answer risk tolerance, objectives, experience questions saying he'll invest in short-term US govt securities initially; Sim uses his fact sheet and opens the account. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: There is no violation because short-term government securities carry effectively no risk of unsuitability.
options.B: Standard III(C) Suitability — Sim did not complete the required section on Hie's investment experience, objectives, and risk tolerance of new clients.
options.C: Account-opening documentation is an administrative matter outside the Standards.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Suitability information — experience, objectives, risk tolerance — is required AT INCEPTION regardless of how safe the initial investment seems.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Standard III(C) Suitability — Sim did not complete the required section on Hie's investment experience, objectives, and risk tolerance of new clients; the information Hie provided was NOT SUFFICIENT for Sim to develop an appropriate IPS; the standard requires when an advisory relationship exists, members and candidates must GATHER CLIENT INFORMATION at the inception of the relationship; a client's statement regarding his or her desired investment strategy, especially without a fully formed IPS, does not meet the requirements of III(C).

Correct. Per GHB Case Solution: 'Sim did not complete the required section on Hie's investment experience, objectives, and risk tolerance of new clients; the information Hie provided was not sufficient for Sim to develop an appropriate IPS. Sim violated Standard III(C) Suitability. The standard requires that when an advisory relationship exists, members and candidates must gather client information at the inception of the relationship.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The incomplete new-account information is a III(C) failure, not paperwork trivia.

questions.stem: *GHB Case — first course of action after Yousoff dismisses Sim's money-laundering concerns*: Sim believes Hie may be using account to launder money; Yousoff dismisses concerns. Sim should:

options.A: Contact the regulator in her jurisdiction.
options.B: Take no action since she has informed Yousoff of her concerns.
options.C: Contact GHB's compliance department.

rationales.A: Regulator not first step.
rationales.B: Insufficient action.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Contact GHB's compliance department — per guidance to Standard I(A) Knowledge of the Law: 'When in doubt about the appropriate action to undertake, it is recommended that a member or candidate seek the advice of compliance personnel or legal counsel concerning legal requirements. If a potential violation is being committed by a fellow employee, it may also be prudent for the member or candidate to seek the advice of the firm's compliance department or legal counsel.'

Correct. Per GHB Case Solution: 'The guidance for Standard I(A) Knowledge of the Law states, "When in doubt about the appropriate action to undertake, it is recommended that a member or candidate seek the advice of compliance personnel or legal counsel concerning legal requirements. If a potential violation is being committed by a fellow employee, it may also be prudent for the member or candidate to seek the advice of the firm's compliance department or legal counsel."'

questions.stem: *GHB Case — Sim recommending oil-linked structured note to Madam Tan*: Madam Tan (70, retired seamstress) wants to invest in conservative equities for grandchildren's education; heard about oil-linked structured note from friends; Sim summarizes verbally; Madam Tan admits she is unsure what oil futures are or how they work but decides to put $50,000 in. *Most likely* violates:

options.A: Standard III(C) Suitability — structured notes are UNSUITABLE for Madam Tan.
options.B: no violation — Madam Tan asked.
options.C: no violation — 10% warning.

rationales.A:
